title: Schema evolution in Avro, Protocol Buffers and Thrift
author: Martin Kleppmann
note: The DDIA author's byte-level walkthrough of how the same example record is encoded in Thrift, Protobuf, and Avro, and why each format's design dictates its schema evolution rules.

title: Language Guide (proto 3)
author: Protocol Buffers team, Google
note: The authoritative proto3 reference; its 'Updating A Message Type' section codifies which changes are wire-safe and why deleted field numbers must be reserved, never reused.

title: Apache Avro 1.11.1 Specification
author: Apache Avro project
note: The official spec whose 'Schema Resolution' section defines the exact matching rules for reconciling a writer's schema with a reader's schema, including default values and union handling.

title: Schema Evolution and Compatibility for Schema Registry
author: Confluent
note: Defines backward, forward, full, and transitive compatibility modes with per-format matrices for Avro, Protobuf, and JSON Schema, plus how each mode constrains producer/consumer upgrade order.

title: Introduction to gRPC
author: gRPC project
note: Official primer on gRPC's service-definition model: clients call methods on a generated stub while Protocol Buffers serve as both the IDL and the wire format.

title: gRPC vs REST: Understanding gRPC, OpenAPI and REST and when to use them in API design
author: Martin Nally (Google Cloud / Apigee)
note: A clear-eyed comparison from an Apigee API designer arguing that REST, gRPC, and OpenAPI embody three distinct API models, with concrete guidance on when each trade-off wins.

title: The definitive guide to Durable Execution
author: Tom Wheeler (Temporal)
note: Explains durable execution as 'crash-proof' execution that virtualizes a workflow across processes and machines, so a crash mid-flow resumes from recorded history rather than losing state.

title: Deserialization Cheat Sheet
author: OWASP
note: Security backdrop for the chapter's warning about language-built-in serialization: deserializing untrusted native formats (Java, Python pickle, etc.) can yield remote code execution.
